title: The Fable 5 Crisis Continues
dek: The weekend's Anthropic-vs-White House standoff over Fable 5 grinds into Monday with no resolution — and NLW walks through the dueling press releases, the Amazon jailbreak report, the wellness-retreat meme, and the dawning realization that this fight was never going to be settled by engineers.

oneIdea.headline: The Fable 5 fight won't be resolved by engineers — it's interpersonal.
oneIdea.summary: A jailbreak report from Amazon escalated into a Commerce Department export ban that forced Anthropic to pull Fable and Mythos entirely. But the more NLW digs into the dueling accounts, the clearer it gets: the technical merits barely matter. The administration felt Anthropic wasn't taking it seriously; Anthropic thought it could simply reason the White House into agreement. It can't. Anthropic is no longer a scrappy startup — it's one of two leaders in the most consequential industry of the era, and it has to play ball with the government it has, not the one it wishes it had.

gpCorner.note: AI commentary for UK general practice; not from the podcast

items.title: When 'safety' blocks the word cancer
items.body: The most useful detail for a clinician was buried in the row: Fable's guardrails were reportedly so broad they blocked questions about mitochondria and any prompt containing the word 'cancer.' That is the practical face of heavy safety tuning — a model that refuses legitimate medical queries is worse than useless at the point of care. Before trusting any AI tool clinically, test it on real consultation language — cancer, overdose, self-harm, controlled-drug doses — and prefer tools tuned for healthcare over consumer models with blunt filters.

items.title: This week's lesson: the risk is political, not just technical
items.body: A frontier model was pulled in ninety minutes over politics and personalities, not a demonstrated safety failure. For a practice that means the threat to a tool you rely on may have nothing to do with how well it works. If NLW is right that the labs are load-bearing, that concentration is itself systemic risk; if Zitron is right that OpenAI's losses jumped roughly eightfold last year, the more likely failure is financial. The precaution is the same either way: a manual fallback and the ability to move providers.

items.title: Don't outsource judgement to conviction
items.body: A recurring theme was the danger of people convinced they alone understand the remedy. It transfers cleanly to clinical AI: a tool's confident tone is not evidence it is right. Keep the clinician, not the model, accountable for the decision — and treat any 'it's safe, trust us' assurance, from vendor or regulator, as a claim to be tested rather than accepted.

the_other_side.source: OpenAI Losses Increased Nearly 8X in 2025, With Spending Hitting $34 Billion
the_other_side.text: Today's brief leans on the premise that these labs are precious, load-bearing assets — Adam Thierer's line that roughly half of last year's stock-market gains rode on AI, and NLW's recurring claim that the economy now rests on them. In a same-day exclusive, Zitron reports the opposite picture from the accounts: OpenAI's losses widened nearly eightfold in 2025 as spending reached thirty-four billion dollars. On his reading, the thing Washington just fought over, and that markets price as inevitable, is an unprofitable money-incinerator sustained only by ever-larger capital raises. The power NLW treats as nation-state-scale, Zitron treats as financial fragility dressed up as destiny.

the_truth_beneath.text: Strip the rhetoric and both men orbit one variable: whether these labs' dominance is durable economic power or capital-dependent fragility. They accept the same facts — the labs are now central enough that the White House intervenes and markets ride on them, AI drove a large share of recent gains, and OpenAI's spending is colossal. The empirical question is whether that spending converts to a profit path or the losses keep compounding; the observable that settles it is audited financials, with the IPO filings forcing disclosure. For NLW to be vindicated, revenue must scale and the buildout self-fund, so 'the economy rests on this' becomes strength rather than exposure. For Zitron, the losses must keep widening until the raises stop. Both are polemicists — NLW sells optimism and the anti-doom case, Zitron sells bubble-certainty and anti-hype — so the signal is where they agree despite opposite priors: both now call the sector's financial footing precarious. Watch OpenAI's next raise and its audited numbers.

headlines.headline: The admin feels this issue, while serious, should be easily resolved. The ball is in Anthropic's court.
headlines.attribution: David Sacks, in a public post
headlines.summary: Former AI czar David Sacks published a lengthy account framing Anthropic as the holdout: a trusted partner found a jailbreak of Fable's guardrails, the admin asked Dario to fix it or pull the model, and he refused. Sacks cast the export control as a reluctant last resort.

headlines.headline: Sacks's post reads like a press release setting up Dario as the sacrificial lamb
headlines.summary: NLW notes Sacks paints Anthropic as hypocritical — the safety company not taking a safety issue seriously — and pointedly shifts from 'Anthropic' to naming Dario Amodei personally. No resignation calls yet, but singling out Dario at least hints at a second way out of the standoff.

headlines.headline: More likely story: the jailbreak wasn't super serious, and the government used the opportunity to punish and humiliate Anthropic.
headlines.summary: AI entrepreneur Eric Voorhees offered the skeptics' counter-narrative: anyone who's received bug reports knows the type, Anthropic thought the halt order was absurd, and the feds seized on prior grievances. 'Anthropic has more credibility on such topics than Washington.'

headlines.headline: Anthropic's defense: a narrow jailbreak isn't a universal one
headlines.summary: Anthropic argued the bypass shared with them was specific and discrete, not a universal jailbreak that strips all guardrails — and that perfect jailbreak resistance isn't possible today. NLW notes the guardrails were so broad they blocked questions about mitochondria and any prompt with the word 'cancer,' so a 'jailbreak' could be trivial or catastrophic.

headlines.headline: Amazon was the unnamed 'trusted partner'
headlines.summary: Multiple outlets identified Amazon as the company that reported the jailbreak to the government Thursday night, showing how it accessed portions of the Mythos model. Anthropic says it notified the government multiple times before the June 9 release with no objections.

headlines.headline: Andy Jassy and Scott Bessent were the central figures
headlines.summary: The Wall Street Journal reports the decision to shut Fable down followed conversations between Amazon CEO Andy Jassy and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent. Officials fielded calls from at least five other companies, but reporting suggests the ban rested almost solely on Amazon's report.

headlines.headline: Still a long way from dangerous cybersecurity information.
headlines.attribution: Andrew Morris, founder of GrayNoise Intelligence
headlines.summary: GrayNoise Intelligence founder Andrew Morris said Amazon's researchers got Fable to discuss bugs in at least four platforms — normally blocked — but that the info was far from dangerous and available from many other models. The unique risk would be turning vulnerabilities into working exploit code, which researchers reportedly never demonstrated.

headlines.headline: The timeline: 90 minutes to comply, no details on the threat
headlines.summary: Anthropic says it got a 1pm call, was told it had 90 minutes to take Fable and Mythos down over a 'national security threat,' but received no specifics. Formal export-control notice came at 5:30pm; the models went dark around 10pm.

headlines.headline: President Trump later signed off on the action despite reservations about its hindering innovation.
headlines.summary: Trump's name was conspicuously absent from the list of decision-makers, which included Bessent, Chief of Staff Susie Wiles, Cyber Director Sean Cairncross, and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick. NLW argues Trump now looks like the only primarily innovation-concerned actor left in the White House.

headlines.headline: Two incompatible stories: 'begging for hours' vs. a flat deadline
headlines.summary: A senior White House official told Politico export controls were 'a last resort after begging them for hours to work with us.' A source close to Anthropic flatly disputed it: 'There was never any begging or asking us to work with us, just a declared ninety-minute deadline.'

headlines.headline: The crux of the issue was the lack of seriousness that Anthropic was applying to it.
headlines.attribution: A White House official, to Politico
headlines.summary: A Politico White House source argued that had Anthropic moved to fix or pause access rather than dismissing the report as isolated, the ban never would have happened. Anthropic's calm, rational explanation read in the room as not taking the threat seriously.

headlines.headline: Can we stop calling an LLM finding bugs in a code base it has access to a jailbreak?
headlines.summary: Software engineer Cory Ward argued the guardrails were never meant to stop finding or fixing bugs, only to prevent identifying and weaponizing new exploits. 'There is nothing for Anthropic to actually resolve... It is entirely down to politics.'

headlines.headline: Sounds like some folks at the White House were unaware that Fable has greater than zero cyber abilities.
headlines.summary: AI policy researcher Miles Brundage suggested officials thought something unsurprising was surprising — and that no domain experts at CAISI or the NSA appear to have been looped in. Colin Kremerer added that many knowledgeable White House tech people have left, leaving the room out of its depth.

headlines.headline: The wellness-retreat claim becomes the war's flashpoint
headlines.summary: White House sources said Amodei was unreachable at a wellness retreat; Anthropic and reporter Ashley Vance, who was at HQ that day, called it false. Vance: 'The feds seem to be scrambling to try and make an example of Anthropic again. This is not technical, it's petty.'

headlines.headline: This seemingly minor detail is what Scott Adams would have called a linguistic kill shot.
headlines.summary: Jeff Cafe argued the 'wellness retreat' line was a sticky, image-generating idea no one can unsee — Dario in a bathrobe with cucumbers on his eyes as someone delivers the news. A reminder that the fight is being waged in memes as much as memos.

headlines.headline: A late-breaking 'it was China all along' theory
headlines.summary: Semafor reported the export controls were imposed partly over suspicions a China-linked group had accessed Mythos, citing 'a person familiar.' The article carried almost no detail, and Anthropic said the White House never raised China in discussions and that its models are already blocked there.

headlines.headline: Three months ago, Department of War kicked Anthropic out of our building forever. Every passing day proves why that was the right move.
headlines.summary: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's tweet added fuel to the theory that the crisis is personal — despite Sacks insisting it isn't. NLW's point: even if making consequential policy on the basis of who likes whom is abhorrent, that's the world Anthropic now has to operate in.

headlines.headline: I respect this alignment and I fear it.
headlines.summary: Ben Thompson's 'Anthropic's Safety Superpower' argued every contested Fable decision traces to safety because Anthropic genuinely believes it alone takes superintelligence seriously. He respects how effective that conviction is — and fears people convinced they know what humanity needs building technology that could rival nation-states.

headlines.headline: When people decide they alone know the remedy, things go bad fast
headlines.summary: NLW says he's seen this dynamic up close: the moment people convince themselves they're the only ones sufficiently concerned and the only ones with the right fix, danger follows — and the stakes are radically amplified by the power inherent in superintelligence.

headlines.headline: Like the FDA demanding everyone stop drinking milk — if milk was 50% of last year's stock-market gains.
headlines.summary: RSI's Adam Thierer argued that whatever got us here, the policy is disastrous on the merits: a leading US AI company forced to pull a product millions used over non-public, unexplained concerns. He framed it as a major escalation in centralizing control over advanced computation — by an administration that called winning the AI race a priority.

headlines.headline: Anthropic dispatches its top security researchers to DC
headlines.summary: The WSJ reports Anthropic sent senior technical staff — Nicholas Carlini, risk-evaluation lead Logan Graham, and head of safeguards David Orr — to meet government security experts and de-escalate. Separately, cybersecurity leaders led by ex-Facebook CSO Alex Stamos published an open letter urging Lutnick and Cairncross to lift the directives.

headlines.headline: If Dario's not on the plane, nothing will change
headlines.summary: NLW's read: the resolution won't be primarily technical, it'll be interpersonal. Anthropic thought it could reason the White House into its view; it can't. Investor Melinda Chu put the bottom line bluntly — sending senior staff won't matter unless Amodei himself shows up.
